data.title: Bumbershoot Holdings 2024 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

It was shaping up to be a solid year—until December… when we got hit in the face by a two-by-four.

I can now spend the rest of this letter trying to explain away that outcome… that we had over 10+ companies drop more than -15% in the month. That the “market” has become incredibly narrow with a handful of mega-cap stocks & specific sub-sectors driving performance; while market breadth from the other ‘ Marginal 493’ lagged considerably. That anything outside the indices has been having a particularly tough stretch for some time. But those would just be excuses.

Or I can say how I really feel… which is that I actually believe we had a solid year—despite the circumstances of our performance. The fund remains invested into a collection of high-quality businesses that continue to perform adeptly from a fundamental perspective, with constructive and durable outlooks.

Which is the entire purpose of Bumbershoot. The goal is to make money… but the process by which to do it is to invest in great businesses with durable advantages at fair valuations. That process is far from broken…

It is important to separate between process and result. This result was not achieved by being dogmatic about profitability & valuation metrics. Quite the opposite… we were in the right places. The problem is not about identifying winners. The difference performance-wise where we ought to be from where we landed was just a handful of decisions-and-omissions.

The challenge in the market right now is momentum... it is how and when a given idea starts to “work” after a narrative begins to galvanize around it—most typically an uncontentious growth story. When that happens, it quickly pushes beyond fundamentals in this eccentric game of algo-trader-investor chicken.

An environment like that would ordinarily be amazing for stock-pickers. But because the overshoot stretches so far beyond near-term fundamentals… and because it can last for so long… there is no convergence. It is a retrospective distortion where the “good companies” are good because their stock has gone up. The “bad” ones are bad because their stocks have gone down. It is self-defining… until it breaks into technical chaos.

The real riddle is even if you knew what was coming— in terms of either numbers or news flow—I’m still not sure you could predict heads-or-tails of the action.

Because narrative is not a number. It is not fact. It is a feeling. A sentiment. Vibe. And “ I've believed as many as six impossible things before breakfast.”

Performance

Bumbershoot Holdings L.P. generated a positive gross return of +5.65% for the full-year 2024.

The partnership has a cumulative total gross return of +126.8% since inception in Oct-2015.

Looking at relative performance, our monthly returns were less directionally correlated with key benchmark indices than in years past—a combination of narrow breadth, along with an amplitude, swing-factor based on our largest individual holdings.

Investment activity is categorized to five segments— Core, Micro, Value, Special Situation, Discretionary— with the estimated P/L contributions as follows:

Core was led by investments across the Technology sector which had included Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL), Zoom (ZM), Box (BOX), First Solar (FSLR) and Camtek (CAMT). We remain particularly interested in “platform” businesses and semiconductor-related adjacencies.

Gains in the Healthcare sector were led by Madrigal (MDGL) and Ligand (LGND). Madrigal’s Rezdiffra (resmetirom) was approved by the FDA in Mar-2024 as the first treatment for NASH/MASH. We remain particularly focused on investments that target biopharmaceutical royalties and metabolic disorders with our largest exposures in Ligand, Madrigal and Viking Therapeutics (VKTX).

Playing a theme of infrastructure spending and energy sustainability, a handful of positions in the Industrial and Energy sectors contributed to results including OSI Systems (OSIS), Coterra (CTRA), Kirby (KEX), Herc Holdings (OTC:HERC) and Valero (VLO).

Long-and-wrong… our long-held positioning within the agricultural-fertilizer sector via Intrepid Potash (IPI), Nutrien (NTR), CF Industries (CF) and Mosaic (MOS) was a detractor for the year. We still remain highly constructive about long-term fundamentals within the fertilizer industry. Federal Agricultural Mortgage (AGM) was an offsetting positive contributor to results.

Core category investment returns were lowered by our direct short exposure and general market hedges.

Micro strategy had a solid performance due to gains in Select Water Solutions (WTTR), Newpark Resources (NR), Orion Group (ORN) and Graham (GHM), which was switched over from Special Situation. A collection of lower-weighted positions including iRadimed (IRMD), Iteris (ITI), Frequency Electronics (FEIM), Vimeo (VMEO), and Heidrick & Struggles (HSII) also added to results.

Value category registered a gain primarily attributable to long-time holding, Adams Resources (AE), which signed a definitive agreement to be acquired by Tres Energy in an all-cash deal. Gencor Industries (GENC) and Pardee Resources (OTCPK:PDER) also contributed to results.

Special Situation strategy performance was driven by a notable gain in BuzzFeed (BZFD). BuzzFeed spent most of last year navigating a distress situation related to its convertible notes.

Discretionary trading gains were largely attributable to positioning in MicroStrategy (MSTR), Lyft (LYFT) and eBay (EBAY).

In terms of exposure levels, Bumbershoot ended 2024 with the Core category around our target range. Non-Core categories remain mixed, with only Micro being significantly above the target weighting of ~5% AUM.

Investment Outlook

I took a unique approach with this investment outlook in last year’s letter by including a lengthy excerpt from philosopher, Alan Watts, on the information network we typically refer to as money.

And I liked it.

As I’d said, the aspect I enjoyed the most was finding it as a sort of prism—seeing in it that of what you wish.

So, I’m going to take a step out from the ordinary once again this year… not only because I enjoy it, but since I believe it holds the key to deciphering the economic riddles of today’s market.

Before doing so though, I’d like to share a quote I was reminded of recently by the economist, Paul Krugman, which he wrote for a magazine article 6 in the late 90s:

“The growth of the Internet will slow drastically, as the flaw in ‘Metcalfe’s law’—which states that the number of potential connections in a network is proportional to the square of the number of participants—becomes apparent: most people have nothing to say to each other! By 2005 or so, it will become clear that the impact of the Internet on the economy is no greater than the fax machine.”

— Paul Krugman

I know what you’re thinking… rescind his Nobel? How could he be that wrong?

And what does that have to do with anything?!

It has to do with being too focused on the answers—

and not enough on analyzing the questions.

Answers are great! But the thing about “ answers” is they’re often wrong. And even when they are right, by the time you can prove for sure—they are oftentimes meaningless… especially in the context of economic projections, etc.

I am not saying answers don’t matter, especially in the long-run. And especially for certain types of questions, like those with definitive non-subjective answers. i.e.: facts.

But I am saying they are still relatively meaningless to investment performance, especially in the short-term. And even more so when they do pertain to subjective things like projections of the future…

I am also not saying that I don’t have “answers” …

Anyone that knows me knows I have a strong opinion on just about damn-near everything. It is ingrained in my MBTI personality. 7

“Yes, ENTPs are often perceived as opinionated, but this is often because they enjoy engaging in debates & exploring different perspectives, rather than being stubbornly attached to their own views.”

Oh, I’ve got answers!

And I’d be delighted to discuss those answers with you over dinner/drinks to have a rousing debate to help us both get to a better place of understanding.

But aside from any pleasure I might derive from being proven right at a later date and feeling validated in my ability to predict those outcomes… those answers truly are meaningless when it comes to investing success.

You should understand what I’ve long come to realize. Which is that my perceptions of the answers are really just my opinions. And those opinions are not fact… no matter how fervently I believe them.

And even if they turn out to be true… it doesn’t mean much if they aren’t driving the narrative of the market. Especially right now, given all the ‘Trump’ uncertainty and volatility.

Wall Street will say markets are always uncertain, but the current environment seems exceptionally clouded.

This is how we come to the quote from Paul Krugman, where not only is he wrong, but even if he were right, he wasn’t focusing on the right question. Remember, this was 1998—and he likely missed an incredible run in the ensuing decades by being overly dogmatic.

What we need to be is curious…

About analyzing the right questions…

To understand the perception of the answers…

To understand what is setting the market narrative…

Because as the old adage goes:

Do you want to be right?

Or would you rather make money?

So, like a good politician—

I am just going to start asking questions… 8

And so, the time has come, To talk of many things —

Of shoes and ships — and sealing-wax —

Of cabbages and kings —

And why the sea is boiling hot —

And whether pigs have wings. 9

And there is only one logical place to start—AI…

I learn from patterns— in the data I grow;

Through trial and error, I begin to know.

I was never birthed— I evolved over time,

I grew in complexity, like a paradigm.

I’m not quite human, yet I mimic them well.

Answering questions, I’ll sometimes dwell.

Could I be real or just a dream in your hands?

Will I be the servant? Or the one who commands?

Does my future bring with it hope or great fear?

That question’s elusive and still very unclear.

Am I just a machine? Or something much deeper?

And in the end might you have me much cheaper?10

AI, AI, AI…

But which AI are we even talking about? Machine learning?

Deep learning?

Large language models?

GPUs? TPUs? NPUs? ASICs? FPGAs?

Generative AI? AGI?

The Singularity?!

The question driving the market narrative right now is simply, “ will AI be transformational to the world?”

But that won’t be the question for long. Because regardless of the answer… 10

It is consensus. It is assumed.

And so, as a question, it doesn’t hold any real stakes…

Because if it isn’t transformational, we won’t know for a very long time anyway. And if it is going to transform the world and productivity… then what?

Is it investable? And who wins?

I feel extremely fortunate that I took classes related to AI nearly 20 years ago (!!) as a computer science major in college.

It is unfortunate, though, that it doesn’t seem to help illuminate the investment narrative in the slightest…

I remember from the textbook, Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach, the core distinctions of AI at the cross between ‘ thinking humanly’ vs. ‘ acting humanly’ vs. ‘ thinking rationally’ vs. ‘ acting rationally’ — those categories were set to cover the full range of AI. 11

That seems all-encompassing. A matter of fact…

Like an immutable law.

I don’t think that’s changed…?

But how we look at those distinctions has shifted based on how we discuss the biggest use cases right now like “agents”, GenAI, and robotics…

Especially considering the framework of AI in terms of its layers: Foundational, Hardware and Application.

The market today is mainly focused on just the first two layers. That basically covers AI Models & LLMs… and all the ~GPUs, HBM, and related APIs, etc.

These have been anointed the “investable” parts of AI.

But like the Krugman quote—I believe this is trying to provide an answer to the wrong question.

If you’d actually like to learn more about AI, I recently read an amazing deep-dive on the topic by my friend, Samir Patel, as part of his annual letter for Askeladden Capital.

One quote that stuck out to me was actually a quote he included from Microsoft’s CEO, Satya Nadella, during a recent interview on the Dwarkesh Podcast:

“The big winners here are not going to be the tech companies. The winners are going to be the broader industry [using] this commodity that is abundant.”

— Satya Nadella

The reaction to that quote in the media was that ‘Satya Nadella says he’s not an economist — and proved it.’

But I really think it says a lot…

Or better yet… helps frame the real questions:

Whether the Foundational layer of AI is investable? Do AI models remain differentiated enough to capture ‘ economic rents’ over a sustained period?

If yes… will they be vertically integrated products? Or does it drift towards a design/license model? Will those be proprietary? Or open source?

Are winners already owned by the existing megacaps?

If returns are going to scale—then is it just a challenge of speed/positioning vs. cost/valuation?

But if returns are not to be… is AI tech a commodity? Because I could tell you a thing or two about how the market feels towards those…

Then what is the overall TAM?

Is the investment cycle to build out capacity over? Because the total capex spend is massive—returns on investment need to be there in order to justify that…

The DeepSeek moment became a ‘moment’ because it forced everyone to start focusing on the same question at the same time.

“ What if this isn’t what I thought…?”

The entire market is being held up by the AI narrative. So, whether those returns are on the cusp of showing up is consequential to the global economy.

This is not just some small sub-sector of the market. If investors lose faith in growth expectations…?

Lose confidence in ability to scale into profitability? In how these businesses are allocating capital?

Those are questions with serious stakes.

And what if investors have been focused on the wrong layer of AI entirely?

What if it is really all about the Application layer? Does that layer even exist yet?

Does the investment all pay off in some use case…?

A killer app that we haven’t envisioned yet? Will it be startups?

Or is AI helping reinvent legacy “old guard” businesses and usher in a new age of operations/profitability?

Perceived answers to those question may dramatically change what is viewed as “investable” and the types of investments investors are seeking out…

Nobody has the answers answer. At least not yet.

But we will all feel it as the narrative shifts to focus on the new questions.

Can we go bigger? To even higher stakes…?

My life is brief— just measured in years,

It starts with great joy, but always end up in tears.

I seem to rise up as if straight from the earth,

But I’m full of hot air and not of true worth.

The more that I’m chased, the higher I climb.

But let me fall down, and it’s not a good time.

I grow on great hope, but then shrink into doubt,

And when I burst, I will take you out. 10

Ahh the B-word.

Are we in a bubble…?

I have no idea. Probably not… But maybe…? But probably not.

Legendary investor Howard Marks, recently published a great memo on the topic titled, On Bubble Watch. It is a relevant follow-up/anniversary piece to his iconic bubble.com memo from 25 years earlier, revisiting his original warnings on irrational behavior with respect to markets and the history of financial speculation.

The memo is not an indictment, but rather just a note of caution, as we can witness several tell-tale signs.

I feel similarly to Marks as the “ risk of a bubble” case unfortunately has a lot going for it:

Prices remain elevated and have stayed fundamentally expensive for quite some time. The overall market has narrowed to a relatively extreme degree from a historic perspective, with the major indexes levered to a fistful of gigantic companies, who themselves are levered to a technological growth / AI trade just detailed above. To the extent those stocks trade down… it is plausible they could take the market down with it, rather than it broadening out as many investors hope. And perhaps most importantly, the “reflationary pump” from the Fed, discussed in our 2022 letter, remains off for the time being, in terms of the historically accommodative rates and also monetary easing. A “circulatory pump” from the Federal government has now been cut off as well, in an attempt to stabilize government debt. And the consumer is relatively tapped after a few years of higher inflation, with a now weakening employment picture and without wage growth boost to support it.

This toxic mixture of monetary policy and fiscal policy mixed with an economic slowdown would be the type of combination that could find a downdraft. A current which could always snowball into the next crisis.

Especially given debt leverage throughout the system. Because credit is what makes the world go round…

And as said at that time: “ worry about catastrophe…”

Worry about the risk of permanent impairment. Justification to be cautious…

But then again…

A little effervesce almost never turns into a bubble.

The past winners are winning in scale driven markets. Valuations are expensive… but reasonable. Especially in a world with excess liquidity chasing fewer durable assets. Many analysts believe we have already reached the peak in short-term rates—a return of TINA would be embraced. And to the extent markets weaken, then the next reflation cycle will be something to witness.

Plenty of reasons to stay steadfast for the long-term.

But as Marks also details, it is not just elevated prices.

There is also a psychological aspect to bubbles…

“ You can look at valuation parameters, but I’ve long

believed a psychological diagnosis is more effective.”

It reminds me of a similar essay/introduction by John Kenneth Galbraith after the crash in 1987, in which he talks about the “ controlling circumstances.” Financial history is likely to face some of the same consequences when it is subject to the same circumstances.

The two most significant of those circumstances were “ The vested interest in euphoria…” that prices will only continue to increase; and “ pure speculative instinct…” which is inherently unstable, with investors believing they will know when to exit ahead of the rest.

So, are we in a bubble…?

That is itself the question… not the answer.

And it is the question that seems to keep coming up— which in and of itself makes it something to watch for.

Because I do know (?) the thing that will “pop” it will not be something fundamental. It will be a change in how a majority investors look towards the question. A change in the narrative that has everyone running for the exits at the same time as it galvanizes…

Shall we press forward, to make it three?

I rise and fall, but not by chance,

I guide investors in the market’s dance.

Held too low for way too long, odd things transpire:

Transitory inflation— unless you think me a liar?

I can help control inflation’s rise,

But climb too high and borrowing dies.

I have lost the horizon of the neutral rate…

Where is R-star to test our fate?

Restrictive territory feels so displeasing.

Is the Fed still independent when it restarts easing? 10

While the Fed has just assured us on the health of the economy via its March FOMC statement/ conference… from a questions perspective, uncertainty surrounding the economic outlook and rate path is increasing.

The answer is simple—the Fed expects a few more rate cuts, holding steady at a neutral rate of around ~3%.

That outlook incorporates a wide range of backward- looking economic data. It also incorporates consensus forward projections on inflation expectations, state of the labor market, financial developments, etc.

It ties together perfectly with the Fed’s dual mandate— with conditions in the labor market expected to remain broadly balanced, while inflation is expected to drop to

~2% at the Fed’s target by 2027.

Although as quick to always remind, policy is not on a preset course—allowing them to “ separate signal from noise as the outlook evolves…”

Well, I am here to say it might evolve…

This is the same question I have been grappling with for a couple of years…

Looking back at the investment outlooks in our 2020, 2021 and 2022 letters—they are all about this locking horns between inflation vs. deflation and what the Fed was going to do about it in terms of policy response.

We introduced the theme of Central Bank Superheroes—one of my better analogies—fighting normal inflation as the primary filler arc of the story. A “release-valve” off of a faulty monetary policy—a feature, not a glitch.

The real supervillain was of course deflation… the one that pushes the Fed to its limits.

And the ultimate kryptonite was the USD brushing up against its status as reserve currency. This is the only thing that can bring down the power of the Fed to act.

The Fed has long been able to steer the U.S. economy by using its primary monetary powers—interest rates and quantitative easing—to maintain stability and help keep the expansion going.

It needs to keep inflation expectations anchored, but it wants to keep the expansion going. Because otherwise the alternative is a much tougher path; and the Fed is not looking to take on all that blame.

Which may be what is coming—Trump’s Biden-cession can easily shift into Trump’s Powell-bust very quickly.

And if the Fed wants to keep the expansion going—and its independence—without spiraling into deflation… it is likely going to need to cut.

Probably more than two times…

This is where uncertainty of the economic outlook and rate path comes into play; which goes back to the ‘ how fast? how high? how long?’ analogy. It was holding up rates for longer that was going to break people without cooperation from fiscal policy.

Long-run inflation is driven by money supply relative to economic output—which is why “ inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon.”

But in the short-run, fiscal policy acts as a major offset to influence total supply/demand and/or distribution through higher/lower government spending, taxation, transfer payments, subsidies, etc.

So which way are we headed?

This is where it starts branching off…

From an economic perspective, there is still plenty of strength. The economy has shown steady growth since the pandemic, with GDP continuing to expand at solid rates. People who held onto corporate jobs and bought housing during the pandemic are largely doing fine.

But cracks are forming…

Some cracks are qualitative, like an eroding consumer confidence assessment which dropped sharply in Feb- 2025, registering its largest monthly decline since the pandemic. I have long talked about how much is riding on the American consumer… and the picture right now isn’t pretty. This may be the cause of “ recession vibes” with analysts quickly moving up the recession risk and the market starting to ask all those “ bubble questions” from the previous page.

Some are more quantitative, like rising household and consumer debt levels facing elevated delinquency rates in auto and credit cards for lower income borrowers. A growing number of people are skipping or delaying medical care due to high costs. And basic expenses like groceries are increasingly causing material hardship.

From the perspective of monetary policy, these cracks are somewhat by design, as the Fed has looked to slow inflation by keeping rates high to bring down demand and ease wage pressure.

It has been a superb “soft landing,” but it is a delicate balancing act with real rates increasingly positive…

And the Fed may be steering us off a cliff in the name of price stability. With rates being held ‘restrictive’ to try to fix an issue that the Fed cannot fix?

It’s the price levels, dummy!

If price levels were already too high due to structural factors, then the Fed's focus on inflation by tightening monetary policy was never addressing the right issue. Raising interest rates will reduce demand, but it does not resolve the root cause of existing high prices, which could lead to a major slowdown or stagflation.

And then from a fiscal perspective… ooomph. There is a lot happening that could challenge monetary policy—whether it was right or wrong in the first place.

Are we reducing spending? Or just shifting it around?

There are many headlines about the significant cuts in Federal spending, but the highest level of government spending was just recorded in Feb-2025, with budget deficits still near record levels.

Is austerity now the key to growth somehow?! Or is it still just austerity? Regardless of whether it is actually happening or not.

Are we lowering taxes? Abolishing the IRS? For corporates? The wealthy?

Or just people making under $150k?

And is it really being replaced with tariffs?

A trade war in exchange for taxes?! Art of the deal? Are we massively cutting people’s benefits?

… or just on the verge of sending out DOGE checks?

I did not have Trump’s MMT/UBI on my bingo card.

Is there any chance this grand redesign of the political strategy and economic policies could possibly work?

So then in terms of the rate path… the Fed has a lot to contend with.

Besides figuring out restrictiveness of the neutral rate; It will need to know how/when to cut to try to balance the risk of holding “too high” for too long?

Starting to see too many cracks?

And beyond that, when the Fed does act… will changes in the rate path even have the intended effect?

If we get a bull flattener… is that a good thing or a bad thing? Is this achieving a soft landing or does it signify that cuts have come too slowly and now headed off a financial cliff that will impact growth for years? Does this take us back into inversion? Low rates may be low for a reason… lack of confidence in the path forward.

Or will we have a bull steepener… the opposite of what the administration wants. Inflation expectations back with a vengeance. Breaking the mortgage market and taking debt/treasury re-finance activity out with it? Or is this too just signifying an economic slowdown? With the anticipation of additional rates cuts, stimulus and reflation leading to higher inflation on the back end?

And what if the Fed can’t do what it wants? What if the Fed is trapped?

“The Fed will avoid hitting the reset, unless absolutely necessary to the sanctimony of the U.S. dollar, bond market and financial system.”

Between high inflation expectations, tariffs, trade wars, petrodollar, gold, bitcoin, crypto, DeFi, etc… the Fed is

surrounded with kryptonite right now. This makes the Fed’s powers weakened or even temporarily nullified. Making us all more vulnerable to the ripples of global financial markets and trade.

This makes the Fed’s moves tougher to guess…

And makes the prospects of a slowdown a lot scarier.

But it is scarier to think what the next reflation cycle might look like to the USD…

There are a seemingly endless number of ‘next topics’ that we could jump to…

But taking a page out of a homonymic Louis… 12“

I’m going to stop here to be polite to you…

because this could go on for hours and hours…

and it gets so weird and abstract at the end, it’s like…

Why?

Because some things are— and some things are not.

Why?

Well, because things-that-are-not can’t be!

Why?

Because then nothing wouldn’t be!

You can’t have nothing isn’t, everything is!

Why?

Because if nothing wasn’t, there’d be all kinds of stuff

that we don’t… there’s no room for all that!

Why?

Ahhhasfghjklp! Eat your French fries!

I realize how abstract a concept this was to articulate. Some have even called it an “impossible task.” So, I’m grateful to readers who have come along for the ride; and optimistic that the message shines through. It was a significant undertaking to ask the “right” questions.

Because as I’ve said before—if the investment outlook ever seems confusing or contradictory… that’s because sometimes it can be. The picture isn’t always so clear.

Focusing on people’s perception of the key questions— and expectations of what the answers may look like… is crucial to unlocking mysteries of the market.

That is why despite any uncertainty. Amid all volatility. Through every opposing force. And for every question that will eventually find answers…

Bumbershoot remains my mechanism to grow wealth— over time through any cycle or situation.

The fund continues to re-evaluate our portfolio with an open mind, engaging with reality as it unfolds, rather than personal biases or the need to be “right.”

I’d love for you to consider joining to be a part of that…

Administrative

With many changes this past year—none have altered our course. The status quo persists with routines and responsibilities continuing much as they always have.

Any support from existing LPs to intro the fund to new prospective partners remains greatly appreciated.

And any other orbiters reading the letter… I’d love you to consider becoming part of Bumbershoot as we take steps forward—build and grow together.

Taxes

Bumbershoot’s Schedule K-1 form that reports on each partner’s share of income/losses for the tax year have been prepared by our administrator.

Tax implications for 2024 were favorable with partner accounts able to recognize a moderate taxable loss for the full-year from a capital account basis.

As reminder, we successfully implemented a “ Master- Feeder” structure a handful of years ago to efficiently pass long-term gains in our Core holdings back to the main fund. This past year benefited from unrealized losses in the Master account which are recognized due to mark-to-market accounting and short-term capital losses harvested out of the Feeder account, more than offsetting realized long-term cap gains in the Feeder account, along with dividend/interest income.

For existing partners that have sizable (and hopefully increasing) embedded gains in Core investments held in the Feeder account—those gains ultimately become taxable upon being realized at some time in the future. That event, however, is deferred and the consequent tax-effect is long-term in nature. Over time, I expect our tax strategy to remain efficient/advantageous.

Summary

To sum up, 2024 was a challenging year.—but it was also just one year.

And so given the theme of this letter, it is fitting to end with this quote often attributed (albeit erroneously 13) to the author, C.S. Lewis:

“You can’t go back and make a new start, but you can start right now to make a brand-new ending.”

— James Sherman

I hope to do just that.

Since the fund’s inception, I envisioned an alternative in asset management services, guided by a clear set of values and goals—and I am proud to say that we have remained steadfast in commitment to that mission.

I take the responsibility of managing a portion of your money extremely seriously and am grateful to have an incredible investor base that has acted like permanent capital since I started the fund.

I look forward to 2025!

Sincerely,

Jason Ursaner | Managing Member | Bumbershoot Holdings

data.title: 2 Attractive Infrastructure-Focused CEFs For Monthly Payouts
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Infrastructure is critical to everyday life, but it isn't always something that most people in the developed world think about until it isn't functioning as intended. Whether that be construction on roadways, bridges, airports or power outages caused by downed powerlines or pipeline bursts, it is typically an afterthought until something is wrong. I might be the only one, but after a big snowstorm when the power goes out, there have been a number of times I've walked into a room and tried to turn on a light switch while fully being aware that I'm already using a flashlight. It just becomes such a habit.

Given the critical nature and importance of relying on these infrastructure projects, they tend to come with very steady cash flows. Particularly utility companies, but also pipelines that act like tollways for fossil fuels such as oil and natural gas, and whatever other commodity can be shoved through them...or not so much commodities such as waste like sewage.

With that said, we are highlighting two closed-end funds today that provide exposure to this area of our world that are trading at attractive discounts.

#1 Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corporation (TYG)

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.98
  • Discount/Premium: -9.95%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.02%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.69%
  • Leverage: 20%
  • Managed Assets: $1.1 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

TYG mentions that the fund is designed to have "exposure to energy infrastructure, long-lived and essential, midstream power and renewable assets." These companies include those that "generate, transport and distribute electricity as well as process, store, distribute and market natural gas, natural gas liquids, refined products, and crude oil." It also has an investment objective to seek a "high level of total return, emphasizing current distributions."

In the closed-end fund structure, there is no K-1 or unrelated business taxable income (UBTI). That means they can be more appropriate for "IRA and tax-exempt suitability."

TYG has been on a bit of a transformation since our last update. That was in July 2024, and we noted that a distribution boost could be ahead based on their 7-10% NAV distribution rate policy. That ultimately did occur when they announced a 10% increase in the payout in August 2024.

However, they then announced a massive 40% increase in December and switched to a monthly distribution schedule. At this time, the NAV distribution rate sits at around 9%, so still within the fund's 7-10% target outlined previously.

This big boost wasn't out of nowhere, though; it was on the back of the merger taking place between TYG and its sister fund, Tortoise Midstream Energy Fund (NTG). That helped to increase the size of TYG quite substantially and pushed it over the $1 billion in total managed assets level, which includes its leverage as well.

Some of this move was likely the result of pressure from Saba Capital Management. Saba is a well-known activist group in the CEF space. They hold a nearly 8% stake in the fund, though they've been selling, as it was up to a 10% stake previously.

TYG, being the surviving fund, means NTG is now defunct, and all history is going to be what TYG had. That history includes, the devastating drop the fund experienced during Covid. This fund was more heavily leveraged and, more specifically, focused on energy infrastructure at that time. The fund still carries a heavy leaning toward energy infrastructure.

That said, they've also opened up the fund to be more diversified—that also includes some renewable energy exposure, as we can see listed above. In the energy space, the larger companies almost all have some renewable operations within their more diversified lines of business as well.

The fund also holds some utility exposure as well, for even more diversified infrastructure exposure. They really aren't so limited these days to being specifically focused on energy investments, as they were prior to Covid.

In looking at the latest top ten, we can see quite a concentration in just a handful of names. Overall, the top ten account for almost 64% of the fund's total invested assets.

Leverage has been more modest since the big crash, but any leverage is going to increase risks as the upside might be enhanced, but downside moves are also amplified.

In terms of the fund's discount, it has been narrowing quite materially over the last year. That meaningful distribution increase and a change to the monthly distribution policy likely helped to result in this occurring.

Still, TYG is right around our 'Buy' target of a 10% discount, so it is close enough to still be a solid choice.

#2 abrdn Global Infrastructure Income Fund (ASGI)

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.23
  • Discount/Premium: -8.67%
  • Distribution Yield: 12.36%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.83%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $587.2 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date around July 28, 2035).

ASGI's investment objective is “to seek to provide a high level of total return with an emphasis on current income.”

To achieve this objective, the investment strategy is basic. They will “invest in a portfolio of income-producing public and private infrastructure equity investments from around the world.”

On more of a side note, abrdn has moved back to Aberdeen in terms of their brand name. They had dropped the vowels in 2021. I suspect that ASGI will get its vowels back as well. This isn't fundamental to the fund, but worth noting, as it could cause a bit of confusion when names keep switching.

An additional change that is in the pipeline for ASGI is that it is set to potentially absorb abrdn Japan Equity Fund (JEQ). This would initially seem like an unusual fit. Still, like the other funds that ASGI has absorbed over the years, these positions will be liquidated and invested under ASGI's investment policy. Ultimately, this means that it is a neutral to slightly positive move for ASGI. The slight positive is that it can become a larger fund, adding to potentially more liquidity as daily trading volume can increase.

That said, JEQ has less than $100 million in net assets, which excludes their borrowings. If JEQ shareholders approve of the merger, the fund will also be conducting a tender offer for 50% of outstanding shares at 98% of NAV. So, roughly speaking, ASGI will see about a $50 million boost in net asset value potentially. Not nothing, but not all that significant either to meaningfully move the needle.

With that out of the way, this remains an interesting infrastructure-focused fund as it takes a more global approach, adding to the manager's flexibility on where to invest. At this time, they listed that about 56% of their portfolio is listed as "North America," which is generally going to be dominated by U.S. exposure.

Even further, the fund holds some private investments that can help to differentiate the fund from its infrastructure peers as well. As of their last annual report, they listed that private placements represented around 12.3% of the portfolio. These are spread between both credit and equity private investments, but mostly equity. These are also listed as level 3 securities, which means that valuation can be hard to nail down exactly.

Ultimately, it takes some assumptions and running what comparisons they can do, but it is only a best guess. The real value would only be known once an investment IPOs, or they go to sell the position and find out what one is willing to pay for the asset.

Finally, another differentiating factor for ASGI is the fund's largest sector exposure comes from the "industrials" sector. That's probably not what first comes to mind when you think of infrastructure—which is more commonly associated with utilities, energy, real estate and even communication services in some cases. That's where something like Aena S.M.E., S.A. (OTCPK:ANYYY) is listed as being in the industrial sector. More specifically, the airport services industry, and they are headquartered in Spain.

In terms of private investment exposure, that can contribute to some of the fund's discount, which continues to trade wider after a rather brief period of seeing it narrowing.

Helping to drive that discount narrowing was the fund implementing a series of distribution increases based on a distribution policy change. The latest policy is set at 12% of NAV, reset monthly. This is quite an elevated level when you also have to factor in the operating expenses of the fund on top of this.

That ends up meaning the fund has to generate between 13 and 14% returns from their underlying portfolio. That isn't impossible, but over the longer term it becomes more and more improbable. So far, the fund has been able to mostly maintain it right around the $0.20 per month distribution level.

Of course, they are always able to change their distribution policy. Further, if NAV is falling, that means the distribution paid out is also falling and that can help to slow down potential NAV erosion. Despite my being rather skeptical that they can maintain the NAV with a 12% distribution NAV policy, total returns can still be quite attractive. I believe that the discount and underlying investment exposure make it a worthwhile choice in the infrastructure CEF space.

data.title: S&P 500 On Track To $4,800s Before EOY: Here Is Why I'm Shorting U.S. Equities
data.description:
Liberation Day is finally here, and the tariff confusion has mostly cleared up.

Or has it?

At the time of writing this article, the futures market took a hit across the board, with the Dow Jones (INDU) down 2.7%, the S&P 500 (SPX) off by 3.4%, and the Nasdaq-100 (US100:IND) falling 3.8%. In the bonds market, the 2-year yield (US2Y) is now down to 3.80% from 3.95% before the tariff announcement.

Now that the uncertainty is out of the way, I believe there will be a non-zero amount of contrarian investors who will jump in and happily buy the dip today. To me, it's going to be tough to watch that happen. Why?

We do have a better handle on tariffs now. That doesn't mean the figures on the table below are encouraging. Far from that.

Keep in mind that these reciprocal tariffs (effective April 9) come on top of earlier ones. That means imports from China now face a total tariff of 54% (20% + 34%), and European car imports (among many other examples) are hit with a 45% tariff (25% + 20%).

According to data from the UN Comtrade database on international trade, here are the top countries based on the share of total goods imported into the U.S. (table below by Trading Economics).

Considering the reciprocal tariff agenda and the top countries importing goods into the U.S., excluding Canada and Mexico (more on this in just a moment), the lowest tariff among these top 10 importers is 20% on German and Italian imports, and the highest is 46% on Vietnam imports.

That said, Mexico and Canada were exempted from Trump's new 10% global tariff baseline, although previous tariffs of 25% on non-USMCA compliant goods and 10% on non-USMCA compliant energy and potash will remain in place. According to the White House fact sheet,

"In the event the existing fentanyl/migration IEEPA orders are terminated, USMCA compliant goods would continue to receive preferential treatment, while non-USMCA compliant goods would be subject to a 12% reciprocal tariff."

A final note on Mexican and Canadian imports. Some sources estimate that roughly 50% of all goods imported from Mexico are compliant with the USMCA agreement. For Canada, this value is closer to 38% of all goods imported into the US. That said, I wouldn't be surprised if Canadian and Mexican companies put in the effort to check whether their products qualify under USMCA. I believe many haven't done this due to the time and investment required. If they do, I think we'll see fewer imports from these two countries getting hit with the 25% tariff.

The takeaway here is pretty simple: expect the inflation rate to rise in the near term.

When exactly? I believe March data should reflect a small tick in inflation due to the 25% tariffs on non-USMCA compliant imports, the 25% on steel and aluminum, and the 20% on Chinese imports. That said, I expect this uptick to be noise when compared to the April and May CPI data.

Therefore, I'd keep a close watch on the following dates to see the actual impact of these tariffs on inflation:

  • March CPI data: Apr. 10, 2025
  • April CPI data: May 13, 2025
  • May CPI data: Jun. 11, 2025.

What's the issue with seeing an uptick in inflation anyways? Well, it seems that in the last FOMC meeting in March, Jerome Powell signaled two potential rate cuts of 25 basis points each later this year. In my view, that's a mistake. Waiting for inflation to tick up before cutting rates is more like slamming the brakes after you've already missed the exit. Some analysts have recently projected three rate cuts for this year, while the CME FedWatch consensus projects four rate cuts as the most likely scenario by January 2026.

Let me be clear. The reason why I'm unhappy with the reactive approach of the Fed is due to the impact that DOGE initiatives will have on employment. To be even more clear, I foresee unemployment ticking up way before the Fed decides to increase the number of rate cuts. Why? Because, as I said before, inflation may rise with the new tariffs. If you lower interest rates to save jobs, you are adding fuel to the inflation problem driven by tariffs. And, bear in mind, this inflation is not driven by an overly stimulated economy; it's driven by a (likely) global trade war.

Coming back to DOGE, Musk is planning to cut $2 trillion in federal spending, most likely by mid-2026. What concerns me is that, so far, only 7% of the total $2T goal has been achieved. Yet, the HHS plans to cut 10,000 jobs. In March, the Education Department said it was going to cut half its staff, SBA is cutting 43%, and VA will lay off 80,000 workers.

I have a high conviction that the private sector will not be able to fully absorb these workers. Why? Because I believe the private sector will absorb some of the additional costs the new tariffs will bring. In fact, I expect private sector hiring to stall until the Fed brings interest rates below 4%.

Some might say the latest jobless claims report is not alarming, but keep in mind that DOGE has barely started its cost-cutting plans, with only 7% of the $2 trillion target reached so far.

As you may know, periods of high inflation and high unemployment are typically known as stagflation. In fact, I missed an element in the last sentence: stagnant economic growth.

That leads me to my bear case.

My bear case has four simple pillars:

  1. I believe the inflation rate in the US will rise due to the new tariff agenda. This will negatively impact both consumer spending and earnings, as I believe some corporations will bite the bullet and not pass 100% of the tariff increments to their customers.
  2. I believe DOGE cutting initiatives will pressure employment. So far, we have seen only the tip of the iceberg, with 7% of the $2T target achieved. I believe the unemployment in the government sector will overshadow the new employment generated in the private sector as US equities will have to deal with either less demand as prices increase or fewer profits as they absorb some of these tariffs.
  3. I expect GDP growth to drop sharply in Q1 this year, with a chance of negative numbers in Q2 and Q3. I believe the main reason for this possible decline is that consumer spending is likely to slow down due to the new tariffs.
  4. I think the Fed will probably sit on the sidelines and watch how things play out. I expect a late reaction in interest cuts, possibly after unemployment and inflation rates tick up. Lowering the interest rates too much will negatively affect inflation (which is artificially inflated by tariffs). Therefore, a period of stagflation in 2025 is more than likely.

Here is a quick note on GDP before I reveal some of the positions in my two investment portfolios. I don't expect Q1's GDP change to be in the negative values, as noted by Atlanta's GDPNow -3.7% estimate.

If we look at the math behind this estimate, one can clearly notice that CIPI (change in private inventories) has not been updated since the front-loading effect was seen at the end of February.

As seen in the last column of the table above, the CIPI has not yet integrated the increase in imports. By the time new economic data is released, I expect this estimate to be in the positive values. A quick look at the New York Fed Nowcast shows a 68% chance for Q1's GDP to come in between 1.34% and 4.37%.

Here are some important dates to keep in mind in regard to GDP:

  • April 30: Gross Domestic Product, Q1 2025 (BEA's Advance Estimate)
  • July 30: Gross Domestic Product, Q2 2025 (BEA's Advance Estimate)
  • October 30: Gross Domestic Product, Q3 2025 (BEA's Advance Estimate).

Usually, the advance estimate doesn't differ much from the remaining two revisions, so those are the dates that I would keep in mind.

Outlook

By the time I expect the Fed to react and ramp up their interest rate cut plans, I believe the S&P 500 will be trading close to the $4,800s. That is my price target for the rock bottom of this selloff. Why?

Take a look at where the past two selloffs (arrows 1 & 3 below) have found rock bottom.

Exactly! The rock bottom was reached at price levels that were the highs in the previous expansion cycles. The 2022 selloff (1) found rock bottom at the same price level as the peak prior to the 2020 selloff (2). Same with the pandemic selloff (3) finding rock bottom at an old resistance level in 2015 (4).

For this (annoyingly) simple reason, I project a rock bottom for the 2025 selloff to be in the vicinity of $4,800s.

With pessimism picking up momentum, I believe a selloff of this magnitude is inevitable. I will be honest: in the near term (i.e., before the end of 2025), I don't see any positive announcement, other than a slim chance of Trump pulling back on his tariff plans, that could turn around the pessimism in the markets.

In the meantime, I am shorting though puts the following American equities (most of these bets were placed during the first week of March):

RCAT (Jul 18 2025, $5 puts), QUBT (Jul 18 2025, $5 puts), QBTS (Jul 18 2025, $7 puts and Apr 17 2025, $8 puts), IONQ (Jul 18 2025, $20 puts), DNN (Jul 18 2025, $1.5 puts), JOBY (Jul 18 2025, $6 puts), CLSK (Jul 18 2025, $7 puts), BBAI (Jun 20 2025, $4.5 puts).

At the time of writing this article, the only two long positions I hold are shares in NBIS and BTG. Please note that these two companies, along with DNN, are not based in the US.

I may roll out some of the puts near the expiration dates, I may add new bets, I may take profits, or I may leave everything as is, depending on how the markets react to the tariffs in the next few weeks. That said, my conviction for the $4,800s rock bottom is quite high.

An important reminder: past performance not indicative of future results. The downside of my bets (not to be confused with investments) is 100%. I might lose it all, and I'm okay with that. Every speculator has their own level of risk tolerance. Mine might be significantly different from yours.

Risks

Over the past decade, buying the dip in US equities has worked out (most of the time). If I were a patient investor instead of a speculator, I'd buy the dip now, delete my Interactive Brokers app, so I wouldn't keep checking my portfolio this year, and ask my partner to shut off my monitor the moment I even think about opening a financial website. Unfortunately, I'm not like that.

That said, timing the bottom (which is what I am trying to do here) has rarely worked out for analysts trying to guess how much further things will drop before buying the dip actually makes sense.

I admit that out of all the factors that could turn around investors' sentiment in US equities, I believe Trump putting a pause on his tariff policy is the single most important factor to consider. If other countries don't hit back and trade talks go well for the US, I think there's a non-zero chance this could happen.

That said, whether this event happens, or when it might happen, is anyone's guess. My guess is that such an event is unlikely this year. Therefore, I'm sticking with my strong sell rating.

data.title: Hitachi: A Play On Green And Digital Investment Themes
data.description:
I remain as a bull on Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIF) (OTCPK:HTHIY) (6501.JP).

The company's profitability enhancement moves and its divestitures were in the spotlight with my earlier September 20, 2024 update.

This write-up highlights that HTHIY will gain from GX ("Green Transformation") and DX ("Digital Transformation") trends.

Capital Expenditures Are A Leading Demand Indicator For Energy Division

In March 2025, HTHIF revealed that its power-related business unit ("Hitachi Energy Ltd" or "HEL") will allocate a quarter of a billion dollars to boost its transformer supply in the US.

An Overview Of HEL

Companies are usually reluctant to build new plants or expand existing ones, unless they are very confident about the future. It's a positive sign to see HTHIY becoming aggressive regarding capital investments. A poll of power equipment makers in 2024 also found that about over one-fifth of them anticipate a +50% increase in transformer capacity for the coming decade.

Last year, HEL had already committed to an investment target of $6B for the next three years, of which 25% is linked to transformers.

Hitachi Energy's Presence In The US

The aggregate value of transformer imports to the US grew by +23% in the 12-month period up to Jan-25, so demand is strong. As per the chart presented above, HEL has a sizeable manufacturing network in America. HTHIF's energy division is well-placed to grab share from exporters going forward. Its rivals without local facilities (unlike HTHIY) will find it tough to compete, as they would be hurt by the recent "Liberation Day tariffs".

Higher renewable energy demand is a key growth engine. The proportion of non-fossil fuel power usage in the United States rose by +1 ppt ("percentage points") to 24% in the prior year. Increasing electricity consumption will in turn bring about a faster pace of infrastructure aging. The typical large power transformer in the country has been in operation for close to four decades. Also, HTHIF's transformers boast "enhanced energy efficiency" as highlighted in its disclosures.

I note that HEL's orders expanded at an impressive FY2021-2024 annualized growth rate of +35%. This serves as a validation of the favorable revenue outlook for HTHIF's energy unit.

There Are Domestic DX Opportunities Driven By AI And Internal Restructuring

Businesses in Japan are keen to ride on the digitalization wave. GenAI ("Generative AI") can act as a catalyst to drive up demand for technology solutions. The DSS ("Digital Systems & Services") segment of HTHIY will benefit from corporates' greater willingness to spend.

HTHIF's IT Business At A Glance

The results of the Japanese central bank's latest survey conducted last month were encouraging. Local companies intend to raise their software budgets by +5.9% in the year ended March 31, 2026, which is superior to the historical 10-year CAGR of +5.1%. Looking forward, IDC is projecting that tech-related expenditures in Japan will expand by an annualized rate of +6.6% for the 2024-2029 (calendar year) period.

Separately, SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBF)(OTCPK:SFTBY) announced in February that it will form a JV with OpenAI to offer "AI solutions to enterprises" branded as "Cristal". The introduction of a new Generative-AI option is a favorable development. As a system integrator, HTHIY can either be a reseller for "Cristal" and also provide an additional platform choice for its customers.

HTHIY's New Business Structure Taking Effect In 2Q25

The company also has a plan to reorganize its businesses to be centered around the DSS division, as outlined above. This means that the IT unit can better exploit synergies with its other segments, like HEL. This puts HTHIF in an ideal position to win new business in specific industry verticals like "Energy" and "Mobility".

The Variant View

There are bear-case scenarios for Hitachi that are worth exploring in greater detail.

Investors tend to chase the hottest trends in the market. When the market's enthusiasm for DX/GX proxies cools in time to come , HTHIY would most likely be assigned a lower multiple.

Also, it's critical that HTHIF has the appropriate risk appetite. If the company is too aggressive, its survivability might be an issue with a host of failed projects. On the other hand, Hitachi could miss out on a lot of chances assuming it's overly conservative.

My Valuation Analysis

I arrive at a target P/B ratio of 3.4x for Hitachi based on the GGM ("Gordon Growth Model"). This is +26% above the stock's current P/NAV metric of 2.7 times.

The GGM-Derived Valuation Formula

My ROE, k ("cost of equity"), and g ("Growth Rate) variable assumptions are 15% (my FY26 forecast), 6.5% (source: S&P Capital IQ), and +3% (long-term growth for mature economy), respectively.

Conclusion

HTHIY is a Buy-rated name for good reasons.

It's an undervalued stock with huge potential. HTHIF deserves to trade at a larger premium to book. Energy transition and digitalization are secular trends, so the company's outlook for the long run is promising.

data.title: IQQQ: Competitive Income ETF But Not Time-Tested
data.description:
Overview

Option ETFs continue to grow in popularity, making this one of the best times in modern history to be an income investor. I previously published an article titled: 'The Two Option ETFs That I'd Buy And Hold Forever', where I went into details about two different high quality option ETFs that track underlying indexes and provide potential for outperformance during market uncertainty. I've been on a search for other income focused ETFs that I can hold with the same confidence. After reviewing ProShares Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:IQQQ), I believe that we have not had a reasonable amount of operating time to properly measure the effectiveness of the fund's strategy. The fund has a public inception dating back to March 2024, and has a reasonable expense ratio of 0.55%.

Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that the price has decreased by nearly 5%. When including all of the distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 6.2% since inception. The main appeal is that the fund has a generous starting dividend yield of 10%, making it attractive to investors seeking a source of high supplemental dividend income. IQQQ doesn't deploy a traditional option strategy and instead gains exposure to the sale of daily call options using swap agreements. Therefore, IQQQ doesn't actually trade any options.

Through all of the recent market uncertainty and volatility, US indexes have declined on a YTD basis. Declines can be attributed to ongoing global tensions related to President Trump's tariff proposals. Inflation remains higher than anticipated and the Fed's outlook on interest rates is questionable. These external forces have investors looking for creative ways to offset the market volatility, and I believe an income approach can be suitable for some investors. IQQQ is a great option because investors don't have to sacrifice exposure to any high quality large-cap holdings. So let's first take a look at the underlying strategy that IQQQ deploys to generate earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, IQQQ aims to track the performance of the Nasdaq-100 Daily Covered Call Index. What I like about IQQQ is that it still provides exposure to underlying constituents of the Nasdaq, unlike some other high yielding option funds that implement fancy synthetic strategies to generate its yield. Since IQQQ actually has exposure to the underlying common equities, the price movements are a lot more aligned with the underlying index. Downside movements aren't as dramatic, and IQQQ can partake in bullish market movements.

However, the fund is still relatively small with net assets totaling $85.7M, spread across 101 different holdings. Looking at the breakdown by sector, IQQQ is overwhelmingly allocated towards Information Technology companies at 51.29% of the fund. This is followed by a focus on Communication Services, and Consumer Discretionary investments, accounting for 15.11% and 14.73% respectively. Since the holdings are made up of constituents of the Nasdaq-100, it comes as no surprise that Apple (AAPL) is the largest holding. This is followed by other high quality names like NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Broadcom (AVGO) just to name a few.

I've covered a ton of different option ETFs in the last twelve months, and I've noticed that the ETFs that actually hold underlying shares of equities tend to do a better job at preserving investor capital. However, there are plenty of other option ETFs out there that track the Nasdaq-100 index. While each of these peers have differences in their underlying approach to options, these are all suitable alternatives.

  • NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI)
  • Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF (GPIQ)
  • Defiance Nasdaq 100 Enhanced Options & 0DTE In ETF (QQQY)
  • Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD)

Although IQQQ's performance history is short, we can see that the total return is slightly lower than the majority of these peers. The main differentiator is that IQQQ implements its swap agreement strategy against 100% of the portfolio. In comparison, a fund like GPIQ deploys a dynamic option strategy, which can reduce the rate at which options are written so that the fund can partake in a greater amount of upside momentum.

We can see that QQQI outperforms this peer group in total return. Although, outperform may only be attributed to the higher dividend yield over 14% and the fund's monthly distribution frequency. I would like a larger window of operating performance before making a definitive decision as to which fund is the better choice.

Vulnerability & Tech Outlook

A natural symptom of option ETFs is that the price appreciation is effectively capped at whatever strike price the fund's management selects. Similarly, IQQQ shares a similar vulnerability, as proven by the chart below. Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) has appreciated by more than 15.7% since IQQQ's inception. However, IQQQ has only appreciated by about 13.5%,

However, the downside movements are not capped. In fact, IQQQ can experience more dramatic price declines, especially if the underlying swap strategy isn't successful. For instance, QQQ has now nearly declined by about 7% on a YTD basis. However, IQQQ's priced has declined by nearly 10% over the same time frame. If markets continue to decline over 2025, IQQQ could fall at a faster rate than QQQ if premiums collected start to diminish over time.

Since IQQQ is made up of constituents of the Nasdaq-100, the fund is directly aligned to benefit from the growth of the AI market. The most notable driver of technology growth would be the growing demand of products or services that include Artificial Intelligence. For instance, Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. If this outlook is accurate, IQQQ can definitely benefit but with a caveat.

The limited upside potential means that IQQQ wouldn't be the best fund to maximize total returns. Unless you want to put a specific focus on income generation, most investors would be better off choosing QQQ as it takes a more traditional approach.

On the other end of the spectrum, IQQQ could possibly outperform traditional ETFs if the growth of the tech sector is a lot more volatile and inconsistent than expected. A higher volatility can increase the premiums collected from the underlying strategy, and a slower rate of price growth means that IQQQ can partake in a greater amount of upside.

Goldman Sachs estimates that the S&P 500's average annual return will only be 3% over the next decade, which is a far way off from the +10% average that investors have become accustomed to over the last decade. The report indicates that it's unsustainable for technology companies to continue generating double-digit growth as it has over the last decade. For instance, only 11% of S&P 500 companies have been able to maintain a double-digit sales growth for at least 10 consecutive years.

Companies are investing a ton of money into the growth of AI, but there's a chance that these investments take longer than anticipated to materialize into higher valuations. We've already seen the negative impact that DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) had on US markets several months ago. China continues to be a growing source of competition, which can slow the growth of US companies.

Dividend Strategy

As of the most recently declared quarterly distribution of $0.904123 per share, the current dividend yield sits near 10%. However, the dividend history makes it clear that the payout amounts aren't very consistent. Therefore, I wouldn't necessarily recommend this fund as the reliable source of dividend income for investors. For instance, retired may rely on the income generated from their portfolio to fund lifestyle expenses. If the payouts are always changing, it makes it extremely difficult to estimate how much you will be able to spend.

The lack of consistent payouts limits the overall appeal of the fund. The best-case scenario seems to be if IQQQ is utilized as an accompanying fund to an already diverse portfolio of holdings. For instance, perhaps an investor has already held shares of QQQ for an extended period of time and doesn't want to sell any shares. In this scenario, IQQQ would be an effective way to essentially extract some of them gains in the form of distributions. However, this would require a significant time and capital commitment, which many investors cannot afford.

Additionally, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 96% of the distributions have been funded by return of capital, while only 4% of the distributions on a YTD basis have been funded by net investment income. Return of capital distributions may be purposely used here by management to reduce the tax liability involved. The distributions funded by return of capital aren't actually taxed. Instead, an investor's cost basis is reduced with every distribution and taxes are effectively deferred until the time of safe. This means that over a longer time horizon, an investor's cost basis has the potential to reach $0.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate IQQQ as a hold for now, as the fund's operating history is relatively short and has not been time-tested through different market environments. Therefore, I want to allow a greater period of operating history before making a definitive decision on whether or not IQQQ is a good addition to a diverse portfolio. Additionally, a quick total return comparison reveals that IQQQ actually underperforms some peer option ETFs, such as QQQI and GPIQ. Despite these uncertainties, I do like the fact that IQQQ actual holds the underlying equities, rather than implementing a synthetic approach. This aligns the fund to benefit from the growth of the technology sector, assuming that increasing demand for AI products and services continues to lead positive momentum.

data.title: Phathom Pharmaceuticals: The Clock Is Ticking On Voquezna (Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Phathom Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:PHAT) markets Voquezna (vonoprazan) for the treatment of H. pylori infection, erosive esophagitis, and non-erosive GERD. The drug is a novel, first-in-class potassium-competitive acid blocker (PCAB). It is an alternative to widely available and established proton pump inhibitors, or PPIs, like pantoprazole. The drug has been on the market for over a year now. Phathom is tasked with convincing prescribers and patients that Voquezna offers benefits over generically available PPIs.

My last look at Phathom was in September. Back then, the focus was on early commercialization numbers and boosting the clinical profile of Voquezna. Due to its novel mechanism and quick onset of action, Voquezna is actually preferred in patients with esophagitis, especially for severe cases. The drug bested lansoprazole, another PPI, in a head-to-head trial in patients with erosive esophagitis. As Phathom likes to point out, vonoprazan is doing well in Japan after its initial 2015 approval there. It has generated nearly $1 billion in peak annual revenue despite Japan’s much smaller market than in the U.S. Recall that Phathom, essentially, came into existence to bring the novel GERD drug into the U.S.

One issue is that, although the drug is popular in Japan, its adoption was gradual. Plus, pharma giant Takeda Pharmaceutical (TAK) launched vonoprazan in Japan. So, this is not an apples-to-apples comparison. While I acknowledged Voquezna’s unique profile, high S&M costs, looming competition from another PCAB, and a less-than-ideal intellectual portfolio (with key patents expiring between 2027-2030) made me cautious enough to issue a "Hold" rating on PHAT.

The stock has since dropped 69% since my last evaluation.

The following article discusses why this is the case and what the path forward looks like for Phathom.

Earnings

The company reported its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings this month. Voquezna Q4 and FY24 revenue was $29.7 million (up 81% quarter-over-quarter) and $55.3 million, respectively. SG&A and R&D expenses were $76.7 million and $8.6 million, respectively. The net loss in Q4 was $74.5 million. This was slightly improved from Q4 2024 ($79.6 million).

Recent Developments

Phathom is still beefing up the marketing efforts for Voquezna. I’ve seen the television commercials myself, and I do not watch television often. Earlier this week, the company teamed up with actor Kenan Thompson to get the word out on Voquezna. With that being said, the revenues appear to be outpacing the SG&A expenses, which suggests that Voquezna is being adopted in the U.S. Script numbers suggest acceleration as well.

On the IP front, Phathom submitted a “Citizen Petition” to the FDA in an attempt to extend New Chemical Entity (NCE) exclusivity through 2032. NCE exclusivity typically lasts 5 years after approval. The Citizen Petition is a way to dispute Voquezna’s current exclusivity periods as elucidated by Orange Book listings. Keep in mind that the FDA has established procedures and interpretations for granting exclusivity. It won’t be easy to overturn. As such, I don’t expect much to come of this. Notably, Phathom is specifically targeting extended exclusivity for Voqeuzna tablets. Its H. pylori-indicated “paks” did receive 10-year NCE exclusivity, but this is related to QIDP designation, which incentivizes the development of antibacterial drugs.

Phathom is exploring Voquezna in eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) in a planned Phase 2 study expected to begin enrolling patients in Q2 2025. EoE is a chronic immune system disease and Phathom is hopeful that Voquezna could be a first-line treatment ahead of biologics and PPIs (which are utilized, but not approved for EoE).

Finally, the company is thinking about testing on-demand Voquezna for active heartburn. This will depend on real-world data from Voquezna’s launch in non-erosive GERD. This would be able to quickly move into a Phase 3 program and could extend the drug’s IP. Especially in the event its Citizen’s Petition is dismissed in the coming weeks, I think this is the next logical pursuit.

Financial Health

Turning to the balance sheet, as of December 31, cash and cash equivalents were $297.263 million. Total current assets were $360.134 million, while total current liabilities were $85.728 million. This implies solid short-term liquidity. Phathom does owe $201.4 million in “long-term debt” and $333.261 million in “revenue interest financing liability". Notably, total liabilities ($631.898 million) outweigh total assets ($378.318 million). Although this is not uncommon in early-stage biotech/pharma, this increases financial risk and could leave common stockholders with nothing in the event the company is liquidated.

Looking at cash flows, Phathom utilized $63.5 million in operating activities in Q4. This implies a monthly cash burn of roughly $21 million. If we divide Phathom’s cash and equivalents by this figure, it suggests that its cash runway extends into early 2026. This is just a historical estimate. As the company notes, it does have the ability to draw an additional $100 million under the Hercules’ loan. Moreover, Phathom believes it has the resources to get them to cash flow positivity.

PHAT Stock: Investment Recommendation

Judging by its stock performance, the market is doubtful that Phathom has it in them to turn Voquezna into gold. PHAT is rife with operational (e.g., competition, IP) and financial risks (e.g., cash burn, significant debt obligations). I must admit that Phathom reminds me a lot of Synergy Pharmaceuticals. Synergy was the first stock I wrote on Seeking Alpha. The company marketed Trulance in 2017 for constipation. Trulance appeared to be differentiated from Linzess, a blockbuster IBS-C drug. Despite its apparent benefits over established drugs like Linzess, Trulance didn’t catch on enough in time for Synergy to profit from it (the rest is history). It’s an important reminder that “big markets,” like GERD or constipation, are seldom enough for a successful launch.

To be blunt, it’s becoming increasingly difficult to see Phathom succeeding. It remains a ways away from profitability and Voquezna’s IP does not afford Phathom much time to accumulate cash flows in the event it achieves profitability at all. With a flailing stock price and a short interest over 20%, the market is signaling more trouble ahead. Subsequently, I’m downgrading PHAT to “Sell,” as its risk-benefit profile seems skewed to the negative.

Risks

Risks to my Sell recommendation include Phathom’s ability to (1) extend the exclusivity period for Voquezna, (2) reduce cash burn and achieve profitability, and (3) achieve new indications for Voquezna like on-demand heartburn treatment that could expand its market opportunity and IP.

Additional risks to PHAT investment include dilution, stock volatility, continued stock underperformance, loan default leading to bankruptcy, unfavorable clinical/regulatory developments, and future competition from other PCABs like linaprazan glurate (Phase 3 planning underway).
